coredata.dc:title: Excitation-independent yellow-fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots for biological imaging and paper-based sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Lihong
Li, Lin
Li, Xiaofeng
Zhang, Guomei
Zhang, Yan
Dong, Chuan
Shuang, Shaomin
coredata.dc:description: We present a facile strategy for fabrication of excitation-independent yellow-fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (y-CDs) and illuminate their applications for biological imaging and paper-based sensing. The y-CDs have been synthesized using 4-amino salicylic acid as raw material via one-step hydrothermal treatment without further surface passivation or modification. As-prepared y-CDs exhibit exceptional advantages including unique excitation-independent emission, respectable fluorescence quantum yield (11.0%), superior biocompatibility, low toxicity, and satisfactory optical stability. Depending on these eminent properties, the y-CDs have been utilized for intracellular Al3+ imaging and paper-based Al3+ sensing. They could image Al3+ fluctuations in living cells without interference of autofluorescence based on their high selectivity and sensitivity detection for Al3+ ions in aqueous solution with a good linearity in the concentration range of 10–200μM and 225–400μM and a detection limit of 1.69μM. Moreover, a paper-based sensor strip containing y-CDs has been prepared for Al3+ sensing, which provides a convenient and user-friendly approach for the visual detection of Al3+.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Yellow-fluorescent
Carbon nanodots
Imaging
Sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence assay of Al3+ in aqueous solution
Preparation of y-CDs-stained paper-based strips and metal-ion sensing test
Al3+ sensing of y-CDs in aqueous solution
Al3+ sensing of y-CDs in living cells
Al3+ sensing of y-CDs in paper-based strips

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Diagram for the synthesis of y-CDs from hydrothermal treatment of 4-amino salicylic acid, along with photograph of the corresponding sample under 470nm excitation.
TEM images of y-CDs (A) scale bar is 50nm, (B) scale bar is 20nm, and (C) scale bar is 10nm (Inset: HRTEM image of y-CDs, scale bar: 2nm). (D) Diameter distribution of y-CDs.
(A) Raman spectrum of y-CDs. (B) XPS spectrum of y-CDs. (C) C1s, (D) O1s and (E) N1s spectra of y-CDs. (F) FTIR spectrum of y-CDs.

(A) Absorption, excitation, and emission spectra of y-CDs. Inset: photographs of the corresponding samples under daylight and 470nm excitation. (B) Fluorescence spectra of y-CDs under different excitation wavelengths.

(A) Selectivity of y-CDs solution (0.25mgmL−1) for Al3+ over other cations under pH 7.0 condition at 490nm excitation. ([Mn+]=400μM, F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of y-CDs in the absence and presence of external ions, respectively). (B) Representative fluorescence emission spectra of y-CDs in the presence of various concentrations of Al3+ (0.05μM–400μM). (C) The relationship between F0/F-1 and Al3+ concentration from 0.05μM to 400μM. The linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and Al3+ concentration in the range of 10μM–200μM (C1) and 225μM–400μM (C2).

Confocal fluorescence imaging of HepG2 cells in the absence (A) and presence (B–E) of Al3+ ions with different concentration (100μM, 200μM, 300μM, and 400μM). Excitation wavelength was 488nm, and emission was collected in 500–600nm.

(A) Images of y-CDs stained paper-based sensor strips upon addition of different metal ions (400μM). (B) Images of y-CDs stained paper-based sensor strips upon addition of different concentration of Al3+.

head.ce:title: Excitation-independent yellow-fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots for biological imaging and paper-based sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We present a facile strategy for fabrication of excitation-independent yellow-fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (y-CDs) and illuminate their applications for biological imaging and paper-based sensing. The y-CDs have been synthesized using 4-amino salicylic acid as raw material via one-step hydrothermal treatment without further surface passivation or modification. As-prepared y-CDs exhibit exceptional advantages including unique excitation-independent emission, respectable fluorescence quantum yield (11.0%), superior biocompatibility, low toxicity, and satisfactory optical stability. Depending on these eminent properties, the y-CDs have been utilized for intracellular Al3+ imaging and paper-based Al3+ sensing. They could image Al3+ fluctuations in living cells without interference of autofluorescence based on their high selectivity and sensitivity detection for Al3+ ions in aqueous solution with a good linearity in the concentration range of 10–200μM and 225–400μM and a detection limit of 1.69μM. Moreover, a paper-based sensor strip containing y-CDs has been prepared for Al3+ sensing, which provides a convenient and user-friendly approach for the visual detection of Al3+.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanodots (CDs), fascinating fluorescent nanomaterials, have attracted increasing attention since they were serendipitously discovered during purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes in 2004 [1]. They are generally defined as carbon nanomaterials with sizes below 10nm and considered to consist of an amorphous or crystalline core with predominant sp2 carbon and an oxidized carbon shell with oxygen-containing groups [2]. By virtue of their high aqueous solubility, tunable photoluminescence (PL), robust chemical inertness, easy functionalization, low environmental hazard, and excellent biocompatibility, CDs have gradually become superior alternatives to other fluorescent nanoparticles. These distinct benefits of CDs make them great promise for a variety of practical applications, such as bioimaging [3–6], ink [7], sensing [8–11], optoelectronics [12], and photocatalysis [13].

To date, there have been remarkable advances in the synthesis of CDs and their synthesis methods can be divided into two major types: top-down and bottom-up routes. Top-down approaches involve laser ablation or electrochemical oxidation of graphite, electrochemical treatment of multiwalled carbon nanotubes, and chemical oxidation of commercially activated carbon. Bottom-up methods include pyrolysis [14,15], wet oxidation [16], hydrothermal synthesis [17,18], and microwave-assisted synthesis [12,19]. Out of these methods, hydrothermal synthesis has been proved to be an effective and convenient way for the synthesis of CDs with its simple experimental setup, easy control of the reaction, and low energy consumption.

Currently, most of CDs show intense emission only in the blue-light region, and the long-wavelength (i.e., yellow- to red-light) emissions are usually very weak. In the case of biology-relevant fields, CDs with blue luminescence might be less favorable as optical nanoprobes because of the commonly blue autofluorescence of biological matrix and photodamage of biological tissues by ultraviolet excitation light. Therefore, the preparation of CDs with long-wavelength emission is highly desirable. Recently, to get CDs with long-wavelength emission, heteroatom doping has been a subject of topical interest. Gong et al. [20] applied an effective approach to prepare phosphorous and nitrogen co-doped yellow luminescent CDs by acidic oxidation of pumpkin by H3PO4 at low temperature. Jiang et al. [21] reported bright-yellow-emissive nitrogen-doped CDs by solvothermal method of 1,2,4-triaminobenzene. Song et al. [22] described a synthetic approach for yellow fluorescent graphene quantum dots using o-phenylenediamine as carbon precursor. Cheng et al. [23] demonstrated a simple strategy for the generation of zinc ion-doped yellow luminescent CDs using zinc ions and citric acid as the precursor via a one-pot solvothermal method. Xu et al. [24] proposed an ingenious strategy for fabrication of yellow luminescent CDs by using glucose as carbon source and concentrated phosphoric acid as dehydrating agent. Chang et al. [25] obtained yellow fluorescence CDs from sucrose by acid carbonization with phosphoric acid. Nevertheless, most of these reported synthesis methods often require multi-step procedure, expensive raw materials, complicated and timeconsuming isolation or purification. Thus it is of urgent need to prepare CDs with strong long-wavelength emission by facile and economic methods.

Aluminium, the most abundant metal element in the earth’s crust, is a common metal frequently used in our daily life [26]. The wide use of aluminum inevitably results in a health risk such as Parkinson’s disease, Kidney damage, and Alzheimer’s disease. Thus the detection of Al3+ is very important from the view point of human health and food safety. Recently, some Al3+ probes have been reported. Chen et al. [27] presented the visual detection of Al3+ using unlabeled gold nanoparticles (AuNPs). Gui et al. [28] reported a fluorescence turn-on chemosensor 2-(4-(1,2,2-triphenylvinyl)phenoxy)acetic acid (TPE-COOH) for Al3+ with a detection limit of 21.6nM. Liu et al. [29] developed a commercial 2-hydroxy-1-naphthaldehyde used as a highly sensitive and selective fluorescent sensor for Al3+ in EtOH-H2O solution. Saini et al. [30] demonstrated a “turn–on” approach for highly sensitive and selective detection of Al3+ using multifunctional salen type ligand as fluorescent probes. However, all these above-mentioned methods suffer from some drawbacks like the requirement of tedious synthetic methodology or the use of expensive starting materials. Therefore, the development of easy and economic method for the synthesis of Al3+ sensors remains challenging.

In the present work, bright yellow fluorescent nitrogen-doped CDs (y-CDs) have been prepared from 4-amino salicylic acid by one-step hydrothermal approach for the first time. The process is simple and efficient. As-prepared y-CDs exhibit strong, stable, excitation-independent, and yellow fluorescence. Coupled with excellent PL properties and low toxicity, obtained y-CDs have been successfully utilized to Al3+ detection in versatile environments, such as aqueous solution, living cells, and paper based sensor strips.

The 4-amino salicylic acid, NaH2PO4 and Na2HPO4 were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Rhodamin 6G was obtained from E. Merck, Darmstadt. 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) was obtained from Solarbio (Beijing, China). Other reagents were purchased from Beijing Chemical Corp. (Beijing, China). Distilled deionized (DDI) water was obtained from a Millipore Milli-Q-RO4 water purification system with a resistivity of 18.2MΩcm−1 (Bedford, MA, USA). Cellulose filter paper (paper strips) with 1–3μm pore size was purchased from Fushun filter paper factory (Liaoning, China). Dialysis membrane with MWCO of 500–1000Da was purchased from Beijing Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China).

The y-CDs were prepared by hydrothermal treatment of 4-amino salicylic acid. 0.2g 4-amino salicylic acid was dissolved in 10mL DDI water at room temperature. The cloudy solution was poured into a 50mL hydrothermal reactor vessel, placed in an oven, and then heated at 180°C for 3h. After cooling down to room temperature, y-CDs were collected by removing larger particles through centrifugation at 4000rpm for 20min and then dialyzing through a dialysis membrane with MWCO of 500–1000Da for 12h.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) study was carried out in a JEOL JEM-2100 instrument operating at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Samples for TEM measurements were prepared by placing a drop of colloidal solution on carbon-coated copper grid and then dried at room temperature. The size distribution of y-CDs was performed by counting over 100 particles. Fluorescence photographs of y-CDs were taken under 470nm excitation using a multispectral fluorescence in vivo molecular imaging system (S-0010A). Raman spectroscopy was characterized using Bruker Senterra dispersive Raman microscopy with laser wavelengths at 532nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were obtained with an AXIS ULTRA DLD electron spectrometer from Shimadzu company using 300W Al Kα radiation. The base pressure was about 3×10−9 mbar and the binding energies were referenced to the C1s line at 284.6eV from adventitious carbon. Fourier Transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was recorded on Bruker tensor 2 spectrometer using a resolution of 4cm−1. The sample with 1mg diluted by KBr (ratio 1:200) was pressed into the disc. UV–vis absorption spectrum of y-CDs was recorded by HITACHI U-2910 UV. Fluorescence spectra were operated with Hitachi F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer. Fluorescence lifetime was measured by the Edinburgh FLS920.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence assay of Al3+ in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of Al3+ was performed at room temperature in 10mM phosphate buffered saline (PBS) (containing 150mM NaCl, pH 7.0). In a typical run, 30μL of y-CDs dispersion (2.70mgmL−1) was added into 300μL PBS, followed by the addition of Al3+ standard with various concentrations. The fluorescence emission spectra were recorded after reaction for 1min at room temperature.

Human hepatocellular carcinoma (HepG2) cells were cultured in Dulbecco’s modified Eagle medium (DMEM) containing penicillin (100 unit/mL), streptomycin (100μg/mL), as well as FBS (10%) and incubated at 37°C in a 5% CO2 atmosphere. The y-CDs aqueous solution (100μL, 2.70mgmL−1) was added to the culture medium (1000μL) at 0.25mgmL−1 final concentration. After incubation for 0.5h, HepG2 cells were harvested using 0.25% trypsin/0.020% EDTA, washed three times (1.0mL each) with pH 7.0 PBS (comprising 137mM NaCl, 2.7mM KCl, 8.0mM Na2HPO4, and 2.0mM KH2PO4). Different y-CDs-stained HepG2 cells were treated with various concentrations of Al3+ (100μM, 200μM, 300μM, 400μM) for 15min at 37°C, respectively.

All fluorescence images were collected with Zeiss LSM880 confocal laser-scanning microscope. The y-CDs were excited at 488nm and their yellow emissions were collected using a 500–600nm band-pass filter.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of y-CDs-stained paper-based strips and metal-ion sensing test
ce:section.ce:para: Cellulose-based filter paper was used (diameter of 25mm) and immobilized y-CDs solution on it. After 2min, the filter paper was removed from the solution and dried in the air. Then, metal ions aqueous solution was dropped on the paper-based strip containing y-CDs and air-dried at room temperature. The change of the fluorescence on the strips was investigated under multispectral fluorescence in vivo molecular imaging system under 470nm excitation.

The successful fabrication of y-CDs was carried out by using 4-amino salicylic acid as the starting material via one-step hydrothermal method (Fig. 1 ). Compared with traditional methods for fabrication of y-CDs using more than one raw material and requiring complicated and timeconsuming isolation or purification, our proposed method only utilizes 4-amino salicylic acid as the precursor which avoids the use of a large amount of strong acid and tedious post-treatment processes. Hydrothermal synthesis involves reactions in a closed system under mild temperature and self-generated pressure, which avoids traditional harsh conditions such as strong acids, high temperatures, and long reaction times, opening a simple and rapid way to prepare y-CDs. To get good PL properties, we investigated the influence of reaction temperature and time on PL properties of y-CDs. As shown in Fig. S1, the optimum reaction condition for y-CDs is 180°C for 3h.

The size distribution and morphology of obtained y-CDs were characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 2 A–C, y-CDs are spherical and well dispersed. The high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (the inset image of Fig. 2C) shows that y-CDs possess the crystalline structure with lattice spacing of 0.28nm, which may be attributable to the (100) facet of graphite. As shown in Fig. 2D, the size distribution of y-CDs is relatively small and the majority falls within the range from 4.0 to 7.0nm. Based on the statistical analysis of above one hundred particles, the average diameter of the y-CDs is 5.68±0.68nm.

Fig. 2 The Raman spectrum of y-CDs (Fig. 3 A) exhibits two broad peaks at around 1351cm−1 and 1569cm−1, which are attributed to the D-band (sp3-hybridized) and G-band (sp2-hybridized), respectively. The D band is associated with the vibrations of carbon atoms with dangling bonds in the termination plane of disordered graphite or glassy carbon. The G band corresponds to the E2g mode of graphite and is related to the vibration of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms in a two-dimensional hexagonal lattice. The intensity ratio of the disordered D band to the crystalline G band (ID/IG) is 0.92, indicating that y-CDs have a structure similar to graphite. XRD pattern (Fig. S2) shows a peak centered at 24°, which is ascribed to graphitic structure [31].

The surface functional groups and element analysis for the obtained y-CDs were characterized by XPS. The survey XPS spectrum (Fig. 3B) indicates the existence of three elements C, N, and O in y-CDs. The XPS analysis of the elemental composition of y-CDs demonstrates that the atom ratio of C: O: N is 4.8:3.2:1.0. The C1s spectrum of y-CDs (Fig. 3C) can be deconvoluted into three surface contents, that is, CC (284.7eV), CO (285.6eV, 286.0eV, 286.8eV), and OCO (288.0eV). In the O1s spectrum of y-CDs (Fig. 3D), two peaks at 532.8eV and 533.1eV are ascribed to C−O/C−O−C and CO, respectively. The high-resolution spectrum of N1s could be deconvoluted into three peaks at 399.6eV, 400.1eV and 400.4eV (Fig. 3E) which should be attributed to the presence of CNC, NH and N−(C3) in the y-CDs. We also used FTIR to identify the functional groups of y-CDs. In FTIR spectrum of y-CDs (Fig. 3F), the peaks centered at 3364 and 3294cm−1 represent the OH/NH stretching vibrations, the peaks between 3028 and 2710cm−1 correspond to the CH stretching vibration, the peaks between 2710 and 2603cm−1 show the CO stretching, the peaks at 1596 and 1471cm−1 indicate the existence of COO−, the peak at 1384cm−1 is associated to the CH bending vibrations, and the peaks at 1259 and 1178cm−1 correspond to CO and CN stretching, respectively. The functional groups identified by the FTIR are in good agreement with XPS.

Fig. 3 Based on the chemical structure of the starting material and experimental results, a possible growth mechanism for y-CDs is illustrated in Fig. S3. 4-amino salicylic acid could assemble into a nanoplate structure through the inter-molecular H-bonding. Under hydrothermal condition, dehydrolysis process happened to form nitrogen-rich y-CDs with abundant carboxyl, amino, and hydroxyl groups on the surface.

The optical properties of y-CDs are displayed in Fig. 4 and S4. As shown in Fig. 4A, the y-CDs exhibit two characteristic absorption peaks at ca. 234 and 282nm, which can be assigned to π−π* of CC bands of localized sp2 clusters in the core and n−π* of the CO bands of carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs, respectively. The optimal excitation and emission wavelength of y-CDs (Fig. 4A) are 490nm and 547nm, respectively. The y-CDs are brown under daylight while they emit bright yellow fluorescence under 470nm excitation (the inset of Fig. 4A). Fluorescence photograph of y-CDs was taken under 470nm excitation since 490nm excitation wavelength is unavailable for multispectral fluorescence in vivo molecular imaging system.

To further explore the optical properties of y-CDs, detailed PL spectra of y-CDs with different excitation wavelengths were also investigated (Fig. 4B). The y-CDs exhibit a strong peak at 547nm with an excitation wavelength range between 420 and 520nm, suggesting excitation independence. Using Rhodamin 6G as a reference, the QY of y-CDs is determined to be 11.0%. The y-CDs also have another apparent fluorescence peak at ca. 462nm, with an excitation range from 330nm to 410nm, indicating the existence of two dominant emission centers. The dual emission centers match very well with the two absorption bands in UV–vis absorption spectrum, in which the absorption band at 330–400nm and 420–520nm might be responsible for the emission centered at 462 and 547nm, respectively. Increasing the excitation wavelength from 370 to 410nm, the fluorescence peak intensity at ca. 462nm decreases while that at 547nm increases. This indicates that the emission centers are in turn excited and the dominant emission alternates when the excitation wavelength changes.

Fig. 4 Fluorescence lifetime is the characteristic period that CDs remain in its excited state prior to returning to its ground state. Typically measured in nanoseconds, fluorescent lifetime is an intrinsic property of CDs depending on the nature of the fluorescent sites and the environment. The fluorescence lifetime (τ) of CDs is assessed by fluorescent decay curve (Fig. S4A). The decay curve is fitted using exponential functions Y(t) based on a non-linear least squares analysis using the following equation: Y(t)= A 1exp(-t 1/τ 1) + A 2exp(-t 2/τ 2), where A 1 and A 2 are the fractional contributions of time-resolved decay lifetime of τ 1 and τ 2. We calculated the average lifetime of y-CDs to be 6.015ns. Fig. S4B displays the effect of pH on the PL intensity of y-CDs. The PL intensity increases with the increase of pH values, reaches the highest at pH 6.0, and then drops gradually when the pH is higher than 6.0. Additionally, PL intensity of y-CDs remains almost constant even after excitation for 90min (Fig. S4C) and storage for 90days (Fig. S4D) which demonstrates the outstanding stability of y-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Al3+ sensing of y-CDs in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: We studied the influence of various external cations with the concentration of 400μM on the PL intensity of y-CDs under pH 7.0 condition, including Co2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, K+, Al3+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Ni+, Ag+ and Na+. As shown in Fig. 5 A, Al3+ is found to be much more responsive to y-CDs than other metal ions. We also investigated the effects of different external anions on the PL intensity of y-CDs, including F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, NO3 −, CN−, SO4 2−, SO3 2−, PO4 3−, and CO3 2−. In comparison with these common anions (Fig. S5), only Al3+ is able to quench the fluorescence of the y-CDs evidently. These results reveal that the y-CDs could be developed as an efficient fluorescence Al3+ sensor. Al3+ is considered to be hard acid with smaller radius and more positive charges [32]. The y-CDs surface is covered with carboxyl groups, hydroxyl groups, and amino groups. O of carboxyl groups and hydroxyl groups and N of amino groups are considered to be hard base. Thus, the sensor response of y-CDs to Al3+ is due to the strong affinity interaction between hard acid Al3+ and hard base O of carboxyl groups and hydroxyl groups as well as N of amino groups based on the rule of hard and soft acids and bases. Such interaction gives rise to the fluorescence quenching of y-CDs through electron or energy transfer. The Al3+ sensing capacity of y-CDs was investigated in the range of 0.05μM to 400μM. With increasing Al3+ concentration, as shown in Fig. 5B, the fluorescence intensity at 547nm decreases progressively, revealing that the sensing system is sensitive to Al3+. Fig. 5C presented the PL data versus the concentration of Al3+ ions. There is a good linear dependence of fluorescence intensity on Al3+ concentration in the range of 10μM–200μM (Fig. 5C1) and 225μM–400μM (Fig. 5C2). The detection limit for Al3+ ions is 1.69μM, which is 4 times lower than the maximum level (7.4μM) of Al3+ in drinking water permitted by the World Health Organization (WHO). The time-dependence of a y-CDs Al3+ solution shown in Fig. S6 indicates that only 1min is required to complete the reaction between the y-CDs and Al3+.

To explore the potential application of y-CDs in living cells, the cytotoxicity of y-CDs was evaluated by the MTT assay in HepG2 cells incubated with y-CDs for 24h. In the MTT assay, MTT could be reduced by the active cellular enzymes in the cells to insoluble blueviolet formazan crystals. The quantitative information about the cytotoxicity of y-CDs can be obtained by measuring the absorption on the cells. We performed the MTT assay in HepG2 cells with concentrations ranging from 0.1mgmL−1 to 0.8mgmL−1. The results demonstrate that more than 85% of cells are viable (Fig. S7), showing the low toxicity of y-CDs to cultured cells under the experimental conditions at a concentration of 0.25mgmL−1. The y-CDs were internalized into the cells possibly by endocytosis mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Al3+ sensing of y-CDs in living cells
ce:section.ce:para: We used HepG2 cells as a model to study the Al3+ dependent fluorescence imaging of y-CDs using confocal fluorescence microscope. HepG2 cells were incubated with y-CDs (0.25mgmL−1) for 0.5h at 37°C before adding Al3+ solution. Confocal fluorescent imaging was performed at an excitation wavelength of 488nm since 490nm laser was not available for the confocal microscope. The results show that the y-CDs-stained cells display strong yellow emission (Fig. 6 A). Obviously, PL intensity of the cells weakens gradually with the increase of Al3+ concentration (Fig. 6B-E). These further demonstrate that y-CDs are an efficient fluorescent probe for monitoring Al3+ ions in live cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Al3+ sensing of y-CDs in paper-based strips
ce:section.ce:para: Inspired by the solution data, the sensing efficiency was performed on paper

based sensor strip. Cellulosic filter papers, which are of low cost and easy to handle, have been used for the study. To check the selectivity, various metal ions aqueous solution was dropped on the test paper containing y-CDs, respectively. Except Al3+, almost no color change of y-CDs stained strips was observed in presence of other metal ions, suggesting the high selectivity for Al3+ (Fig. 7 A). Different concentrations of Al3+ aqueous solution were dropped on the paper-based strips stained y-CDs, respectively. Fig. 7B shows that similar to fluorescence quenching experiment in aqueous solution, the PL brightness of the paper strips decreases with increasing concentration of Al3+. This indicates that the paper based sensor is as good as solution based sensor.

We have described an easy route for fabricating y-CDs with the use of 4-amino salicylic acid as the precursor via one-pot hydrothermal synthesis. As-prepared y-CDs show not only high fluorescence quantum yield and extraordinary excitation-independent emission but low cytotoxicity and superior biocompatibility. Interestingly, obtained y-CDs have been developed as fluorescent nanosensors for rapid, precise, and quantitative sensing of Al3+ ions in aqueous solution. More importantly, confocal fluorescence imaging of HepG2 cells indicates that the y-CDs could visualize Al3+ fluctuations in living cells with negligible autofluorescence. In addition, a paper-based sensor strip containing y-CDs has been prepared for practical and versatile application of Al3+ sensing. We prospect that the proposed strategy will promote the development and application of CDs in biomedical, environmental, and photoelectric fields.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21575084, 21475080, 21306108, and 21305082), and Shanxi Scholarship Council of China (2014-018 and 2014-017), Innovative Talents in Higher School Support Plan(2014107), and Technology Foundation for Selected Overseas Chinese Scholar in Shanxi Province. We also acknowledge Dr. Juanjuan Wang from the Scientific Instrument Center at Shanxi University for her help with Zeiss LSM880 confocal laser-scanning microscope measurements.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.05.065.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Graphitized carbon dots emitting strong green photoluminescence
Eco-friendly synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots from wool for multicolor cell imaging patterning, and biosensing
Green synthesis of carbon nanodots from cotton for multicolor imaging patterning, and sensing
Hair fiber as a precursor for synthesizing of sulfur-and nitrogen-co-doped carbon dots with tunable luminescence properties
Facile and eco-friendly synthesis of green fluorescent carbon nanodots for applications in bioimaging, patterning and staining
Controllable synthesis of green and blue fluorescent carbon nanodots for pH and Cu2+ sensing in living cells
Comparative study for N and S doped carbon dots: synthesis, characterization and applications for Fe3+ probe and cellular imaging
Low temperature synthesis of phosphorous and nitrogen co-doped yellow fluorescent carbon dots for sensing and bioimaging
Bright-yellow-emissive N-doped carbon dots: preparation, cellular imaging, and bifunctional sensing

Zinc ion-doped carbon dots with strong yellow photoluminescence

Rapid visual detection of aluminium ion using citrate capped gold nanoparticles

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Lihong Shi is now working as an associate professor in Shanxi University, Shanxi Province, China. She obtained her Ph.D. degree in Physical Chemistry from Shanxi Institute of Coal Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2008. Her current research interests include nanomaterial and chemosensors.
Lin Li obtained B.Sc. degree in Chemistry from Changzhi University in 2015. Now she is a postgraduate in Shanxi University and major in analytical chemistry.
Xiaofeng Li received BSc degree in chemistry from Shanxi Nomal University in 1999. Now he is a postgraduate in Shanxi University. His research lied in supermolecular chemsitry.

Guomei Zhang received her BSc degree from the Shanxi University of China (Taiyuan) in 1997, her MSc degree and PhD degree in analytical and inorganic chemistry in 2003 and 2006 from Shanxi University, respectively. She joined the Chemistry Faculty of Shanxi University in 2006, and master tutor at Shanxi University since 2008 and has been a professor in 2014. Her current research interests include the DNA assembly and photoluminescence analysis of nanomaterials.

Yan Zhang received is now working as an associate professor in Shanxi University, Shanxi Province, China. She obtained her PhD degree in analytical chemistry from Shanxi University in 2008. Her current research interests include metal nanomaterials.

Chuan Dong is a professor of Analytical Chemistry at Shanxi University. He obtained his Ph.D. degree at Shanxi University in 2002. Dong has published over 200 papers and 100 patents and his current research interests are biosensors, chemosensors, optical fiber sensor, fluorescence and room temperature phosphorescence, and environmental analysis and monitoring.

Shaomin Shuang is a professor of Analytical Chemistry at Shanxi University. She received her Ph.D. degree in South China University of Technology in 1998. She has published extensively in journals such as Nanoscale,Journal of Materials Chemistry B, Chemical Communications, Sensors and Actuators B, Analytical Chemistry, Talanta etc. Her research interests are in the areas of analytical chemistry, supermolecular chemsitry, and photochemsity.
